How Does Your Computer Know What Time It Is?

NTP, atomic clocks, leap seconds, and why your computer's clock is almost certainly accurate to within a fraction of a second — explained simply.

📖 6 min read · Timekeeping

Your Computer Has No Internal Sense of Time

[PLACEHOLDER — Explain that computers don't innately know the time. They have a hardware clock (CMOS clock) that ticks while powered off, but it drifts. Without synchronization, computers lose or gain seconds every day, which becomes a significant problem over time.]

NTP: The Internet's Time Synchronization Protocol

[PLACEHOLDER — Explain what NTP (Network Time Protocol) is: a protocol that allows devices to synchronize their clocks with internet time servers. Explain how it works at a high level — a device asks a time server what time it is, the server responds with a timestamp, and the device adjusts its clock accounting for network latency.]

The Time Server Hierarchy

[PLACEHOLDER — Describe the stratum hierarchy: Stratum 0 devices are atomic clocks and GPS receivers — the source of truth. Stratum 1 servers are directly connected to Stratum 0. Stratum 2 servers (like pool.ntp.org) sync from Stratum 1 and serve most of the internet. Your computer or phone syncs to a Stratum 2 or 3 server.]

How Accurate Is Your Clock?

[PLACEHOLDER — Explain that a typical device synced via NTP is accurate to within a few milliseconds. Mention that the clock displayed on this page reads from your device's system time, which NTP keeps synchronized. Contrast this with pre-NTP computers, which could drift by minutes per day.]

Clock Drift and What Happens Without Sync

[PLACEHOLDER — Explain clock drift: crystal oscillators in hardware clocks aren't perfectly precise. They run slightly fast or slow, accumulating error over time. Describe what used to happen before NTP was widespread — servers with timestamps hours apart, log files that made no sense, financial records with incorrect times.]

Leap Seconds: The Edge Case That Breaks Things

[PLACEHOLDER — Explain what a leap second is and why it exists: the Earth's rotation is slightly irregular, so occasionally an extra second is inserted into UTC to keep it aligned with astronomical time. Discuss why this causes software problems — systems that expect time to always move forward by exactly one second can crash or behave unpredictably. Note that major tech companies have pushed to abolish the leap second.]

See the current time tracked to the second in your browser.
